# Particle-Mesh Methods {#sec-particle-mesh}

Particle-mesh workflows combine distributed particles, distributed fields, interpolation, and solvers. The core cycle is common across plasma, accelerator, and astrophysical applications.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Push["Advance particles"]
  Migrate["Update particle layout"]
  Deposit["Scatter/deposit to mesh"]
  Solve["Solve field equation"]
  Gather["Gather field to particles"]

  Push --> Migrate --> Deposit --> Solve --> Gather --> Push
```

## Core operations

| Operation | IPPL area |
|---|---|
| Particle advance | User container or manager code |
| Particle migration | `ParticleSpatialLayout`, overlap layout |
| Deposit/scatter | `CIC`, particle-field interaction |
| Field solve | Poisson, Maxwell, FFT, FEM solvers |
| Gather | `CIC`, field interpolation to particle positions |

## CIC interface

Cloud-in-cell interpolation currently appears as global gather/scatter functions. The implementation scatters to or gathers from `2^Dim` neighboring mesh points. In 2D, for example, the contributing points are `(i, j)`, `(i - 1, j)`, `(i, j - 1)`, and `(i - 1, j - 1)`.

The PIC test uses the interface directly:

```cpp
scatter(qm, rho, this->R);
gather(this->E, efield, this->R);
```

The first call deposits a particle attribute onto a scalar mesh field. The second interpolates a vector field back to particle positions.

## CIC weights and conservation

For each particle, CIC computes lower and upper interpolation weights in every dimension. Scatter uses atomic adds into the field:

```cpp
Kokkos::atomic_add(&field(...), particleValue * weightProduct);
```

Gather computes the weighted sum of the same neighboring mesh points. The particle gather/scatter tests document three important contracts:

| Test behavior | Contract |
|---|---|
| gather from constant field | interpolation of a constant field returns that constant. |
| gather with `addToAttribute = true` | gathered values can either replace or increment an attribute. |
| scatter all particles | total field charge equals total particle charge. |
| scatter with custom range | a subset of local particles can be deposited. |
| scatter with custom hash | particles can be deposited through a remapped index list. |

Typical calls:

```cpp
field = 0.0;
scatter(bunch.Q, field, bunch.R);
double totalField = field.sum();
double totalParticles = bunch.Q.sum();

gather(bunch.Q, field, bunch.R);
gather(bunch.Q, field, bunch.R, true); // add to current attribute
```

## PIC test loop

The `PICnd` test exercises the following end-to-end sequence:

1. Construct `NDIndex`, `UniformCartesian`, `FieldLayout`, and `ParticleSpatialLayout`.
2. Create a derived `ParticleBase` container and register particle attributes.
3. Initialize particle positions and attributes.
4. Call `update()` to migrate particles to owning ranks.
5. Deposit particle charge with `scatter`.
6. Initialize a static electric field.
7. Gather the electric field at particle locations with `gather`.
8. Advance particle velocity and position.
9. Repeat migration and conservation checks.

The time loop in `PICnd` has the canonical PIC shape:

```cpp
P->R = P->R + dt * P->P;
P->update();

P->scatterCIC(totalP, it + 1);
P->gatherCIC();

P->P = P->P + dt * P->qm * P->E;
```

`scatterCIC` resets the mesh field, deposits charge, reduces the total particle count, and checks charge conservation:

```cpp
EFDMag_m = 0.0;
scatter(qm, EFDMag_m, this->R);

double Q_grid = EFDMag_m.sum();
ippl::Comm->reduce(&local_particles, &Total_particles, 1, std::plus<unsigned int>());
```

This is the right level of example for user documentation because it uses IPPL abstractions without hiding the data movement.

Its default command shape is:

```bash
srun ./PICnd 128 128 128 10000 10 --info 10
```

where the first three arguments are grid sizes, followed by total particle count and number of time steps.

## Load balancing hook

`PICnd` also shows a first load-balancing pattern. It initializes an `OrthogonalRecursiveBisection` object from the field layout, mesh, and field magnitude, then optionally repartitions:

```cpp
orb.initialize(fl, mesh, EFDMag_m);
bool changed = orb.binaryRepartition(this->R, fl, fromAnalyticDensity);
if (changed) {
    this->updateLayout(fl, mesh);
}
```

The important user contract is that field layouts, local fields, and particle layouts must be updated together after repartitioning.

## First examples to document

- `test/particle/PICnd.cpp` for a compact PIC test.
- `alpine/LandauDamping.cpp` and ALPINE managers for production-style mini-apps.
- `examples/collisions/P3MHeating.cpp` for P3M-style coupling.
